sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A cognitive psychology course compared three study strategies for a 40-item vocabulary set. Students were assigned to massed practice (all study in one sitting), spaced practice across three days, or retrieval practice with brief quizzes across three days. Researchers measured immediate recall the next day and delayed recall two weeks later. Immediate recall scores averaged 31, 33, and 35 items, respectively. Delayed recall averaged 18, 26, and 30 items. Students in the massed-practice group reported feeling the most prepared immediately after studying, despite showing the steepest decline at the two-week test.
questions.stem: Which strategy showed the largest advantage on the delayed test?

choices.D: All strategies performed equally on delay

explanations.C: Correct. On the delayed test, retrieval practice produced the highest mean score at 30 items. That exceeds spaced practice at 26 and massed practice at 18, making retrieval practice the strongest long-term strategy in Figure 1.
explanations.A: Massed practice had the lowest delayed score, not the best.
explanations.B: Spaced practice improved retention but still trailed retrieval practice on the delayed test.
explanations.D: The delayed scores differ clearly across the three strategies.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Immediate and two-week delayed recall for massed practice, spaced practice, and retrieval practice.

questions.stem: The pattern in Figure 1 most directly demonstrates which principle?

choices.A: Long-term retention benefits from spacing and retrieval rather than fluency alone

explanations.A: Correct. Students who felt most prepared after massed practice actually retained the least two weeks later, whereas spaced and retrieval practice preserved more information. That is a classic demonstration that durable learning depends on how information is encoded and retrieved, not just on short-term feelings of fluency.
explanations.B: State-dependent memory concerns matching internal states at encoding and recall, which the study did not manipulate.
explanations.C: Punishment is a behaviorist concept unrelated to the study strategies compared here.
explanations.D: The bystander effect concerns helping behavior in groups, not memory retention.

questions.stem: Why might massed-practice students have overestimated their learning?

choices.D: Repeated exposure in one sitting can create an illusion of mastery even when durable encoding is weak
choices.A: Vocabulary items become biologically impossible to forget after one sitting
choices.B: Their hippocampi stop functioning during massed practice
choices.C: The next-day test always penalizes massed practice

explanations.D: Correct. Cramming makes material feel familiar because it was seen recently and repeatedly. That fluency can be misread as true mastery, even though the weaker spacing and retrieval demands produce less stable long-term memory traces.
explanations.A: No study strategy makes forgetting biologically impossible.
explanations.B: The passage provides no basis for claiming hippocampal failure.
explanations.C: The next-day test was not what hurt massed practice; the major weakness appears on the delayed test.

questions.stem: A student who wants the best long-term retention should most likely do what?

choices.B: Spread studying over time and include self-quizzing
choices.A: Read the list once immediately before the exam
choices.C: Study everything in one long session because it feels easiest
choices.D: Avoid testing because tests only measure memory without improving it

explanations.B: Correct. The two strongest delayed performances came from strategies that distributed practice across days and especially from retrieval practice. The clearest application is to combine spacing with self-testing rather than rely on one last massed review.
explanations.A: A single last-minute reading resembles massed practice and is not supported by the delayed outcomes.
explanations.C: Ease of study did not predict long-term success in this experiment.
explanations.D: Retrieval practice itself improved memory, so avoiding tests would ignore the most effective method in the figure.

questions.stem: Which result would most strengthen the conclusion that retrieval practice—not just extra time—improved delayed recall?

choices.C: The spaced-practice and retrieval-practice groups spent equal total time studying
choices.A: The massed-practice group had fewer students than the other two groups
choices.B: Vocabulary words were printed in a larger font
choices.D: Students preferred the strategy they were assigned

explanations.C: Correct. If total study time was equal, then the better delayed performance of retrieval practice is more plausibly attributable to the testing process itself rather than simply to spending more minutes with the material.
explanations.A: Unequal group size could matter for precision, but it does not directly isolate the mechanism behind retrieval practice.
explanations.B: Font size is irrelevant unless it differed systematically by condition and explained the effect.
explanations.D: Student preference does not show whether retrieval practice worked because of extra time or because of active recall.

questions.passage: Researchers compared younger adults (ages 18–25) and older adults (ages 65–75) on a face-name memory task. Within each age group, participants either reread the names three times or used imagery-based elaborative encoding by creating a vivid scene connecting the face and name. Immediate recall and one-week recall were recorded. Younger adults scored 16 and 14 under rereading, versus 18 and 17 with imagery. Older adults scored 12 and 9 under rereading, versus 15 and 13 with imagery. The imagery instructions were rated as effortful, especially by older adults, but both age groups improved when using them.
questions.stem: Which group had the highest one-week recall in Figure 2?

explanations.D: Correct. The highest delayed value is 17, which belongs to younger adults in the imagery condition. No other group reaches that level on the one-week test.
explanations.A: Younger adults who reread scored 14 at one week, below the imagery condition.
explanations.B: Older adults using imagery improved to 13, but that still falls below younger adults using imagery.
explanations.C: Older adults who reread had the lowest one-week score at 9.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Immediate and one-week face-name recall for younger and older adults under rereading versus imagery-based encoding.

questions.stem: What does the pattern suggest about elaborative encoding?

choices.A: It benefits both age groups, even though performance remains lower overall in older adults
choices.B: It helps only younger adults because older adults find it effortful
choices.C: It harms delayed memory because it requires too much imagery
choices.D: It eliminates all age-related differences in memory

explanations.A: Correct. Both age groups improve when they use imagery, indicating that elaborative encoding is broadly useful. However, older adults still score lower than younger adults in the matched condition, so the strategy helps without fully erasing age differences.
explanations.B: Effort does not mean inefficacy; older adults still improved with imagery.
explanations.C: Delayed memory is better, not worse, under imagery in both age groups.
explanations.D: Age-related differences are reduced somewhat but not eliminated.

questions.stem: Creating a vivid scene to link a face and a name is best described as what kind of processing?

explanations.B: Correct. Imagery links the items meaningfully and richly, which is characteristic of elaborative or deep processing. Deeper encoding generally yields better later retrieval than shallow repetition alone.
explanations.A: Proactive interference refers to old learning disrupting new learning, which is not what the strategy is doing.
explanations.C: Sensory adaptation concerns decreased responsiveness to constant stimuli, not memory encoding.
explanations.D: Negative reinforcement is an operant-conditioning term unrelated to this task.

questions.stem: If a clinic wants to help older adults remember new caregivers’ names, which recommendation is most consistent with the data?

choices.C: Teach them to generate a simple image or scene linking the caregiver’s face and name
choices.A: Ask them to reread a printed list repeatedly without imagery
choices.B: Advise them that strategy use is pointless after age 65
choices.D: Have them avoid effortful encoding because effort always harms recall

explanations.C: Correct. Although older adults still recalled fewer names overall, they performed substantially better with imagery than with rereading. That makes imagery-based linking the most evidence-consistent recommendation in the applied scenario.
explanations.A: Rereading was the weaker strategy in both age groups.
explanations.B: The data directly contradict the claim that strategy use is pointless in older age.
explanations.D: Effort and benefit can coexist; here the effortful strategy improved recall.

questions.stem: Which conclusion would be least justified from these results?

choices.A: Normal aging prevents any improvement from memory strategies
choices.B: Imagery improved recall more than rereading in this sample
choices.C: Older adults performed below younger adults on this task overall
choices.D: Imagery may be a useful intervention to test in real-world name learning

explanations.A: Correct. The least justified claim is that older adults cannot improve. In fact, their scores rose from 12/9 under rereading to 15/13 under imagery, showing that strategy-based gains are clearly possible.
explanations.B: This statement matches the observed pattern in the figure.
explanations.C: This statement is supported because older adults scored lower than younger adults within each strategy condition.
explanations.D: The findings make imagery a reasonable intervention to evaluate further, even though ecological validity would still need to be studied.
